1: The measuring rod like a staff echoes Ezekiel 40:3 (the bronze man with a linen cord and measuring rod who measures the eschatological temple) and Zechariah 2:1–5 (the man with the measuring line going to measure Jerusalem). In both OT texts, measuring signals ownership and protection — the measured space is claimed by God. "The temple of God and the altar, and those who worship there" — the measuring of the worshippers, not just the building, shows that what is being protected is the covenant community, not the architecture. This is consistent with Ephesians 2:19–22 (believers as the temple) and 1 Corinthians 3:16 (the community as the temple of the Holy Spirit).
2: "The court outside the temple — do not measure it, for it has been given over to the nations; they will trample the holy city for forty-two months" — the distinction between the inner court (measured/protected) and the outer court (unmeasured/trampled) echoes the architecture of the Herodian temple: the Court of Israel (inner) was separated from the Court of the Gentiles (outer) by a barrier with inscriptions forbidding Gentiles on pain of death (Josephus, Jewish War 5.194; cf. Acts 21:28). The "trampling for forty-two months" (3.5 years) echoes Daniel 7:25 ("he shall wear out the holy ones of the Most High... for a time, two times, and half a time") and Daniel 8:13 (the host and the temple trampled underfoot). The 42 months is the time-unit of the tribulation period throughout Revelation (Revelation 11:2; Revelation 13:5 = 42 months; Revelation 11:3; Revelation 12:6 = 1,260 days; Revelation 12:14 = time, times, and half a time — all representing 3.5 years).
3: The 1,260 days (= 42 months = 3.5 years) during which the two witnesses prophesy is the same tribulation period as the outer court's trampling. The witnesses prophesy throughout the same period that the nations are trampling — their ministry is simultaneous with the opposition. Sackcloth garments are the garment of mourning and prophetic urgency: the prophets wore sackcloth to signal the urgency of their message (Isaiah 20:2; Joel 1:8; Matthew 11:21). The two witnesses' prophesying in sackcloth signals a ministry characterized by lament and urgency rather than triumphalism.
4: "The two olive trees and the two lampstands that stand before the Lord of the earth" — the direct citation from Zechariah 4:2–3, 11–14, where the angel shows Zechariah a golden lampstand with two olive trees beside it representing "the two anointed ones who stand by the Lord of the whole earth" (Zechariah 4:14). In Zechariah, these are Zerubbabel (the governor, representing royal/political leadership) and Joshua (the high priest, representing priestly leadership) — the two leadership offices of the restoration community, both of which are types of Christ (the priest-king). The two witnesses continue this Zecharian pattern: they represent the combined prophetic, priestly, and royal witness of the covenant community.
5: Fire coming from the witnesses' mouths echoes Jeremiah 5:14 ("I am making my words in your mouth a fire, and this people wood, and the fire shall consume them") — the prophet's word is itself a fire-judgment. The enemy-devouring fire also echoes Elijah's fire from heaven (2 Kings 1:10, 12, where fire comes down from heaven at Elijah's word and consumes the soldiers). The witnesses' power is not personal but delegated — they speak the word of God and that word operates as fire. "If anyone would harm them" — the divine protection operates through the prophetic word itself; the weapon that protects is the same weapon that proclaims.
6: Shutting the sky from rain during the days of prophesying echoes Elijah's prayer that stopped rain for 3.5 years during the Ahab-Jezebel period (1 Kings 17:1; Luke 4:25; James 5:17 — Elijah prayed and it did not rain for 3.5 years, the same period as the witnesses' ministry). Turning water to blood and striking the earth with plagues echoes Moses in Egypt (Exodus 7:17–21; Revelation 9:14). The two witnesses thus embody both Moses and Elijah — the two figures who appeared at the Transfiguration (Mark 9:4) representing the Law and the Prophets, and whom Jewish tradition expected to return before the Day of the LORD (Malachi 4:5 for Elijah; and Deuteronomy 18:15–18 for a prophet like Moses).
7: The beast "rising from the Abyss" is the first mention of the beast (Greek thērion) in Revelation — it will dominate Revelation 13–19. Its origin in the Abyss (the demonic underworld-prison) signals its supernatural evil nature; it is not merely a political entity but a satanically-powered system. The beast making war on the witnesses and conquering them echoes Daniel 7:21 ("the horn was making war against the holy ones and was prevailing over them"), where the fourth beast's representative kills the holy ones until the Ancient of Days comes in judgment. The witnesses' death is not a defeat but the completion of their testimony (martyria = witness/testimony → martyr).
8: "The great city that is figuratively called Sodom and Egypt, where their Lord was crucified" — the identification of the great city is deliberately multilayered. "Where their Lord was crucified" is the historical identification: Jerusalem. "Figuratively called Sodom" echoes Isaiah 1:10 and Ezekiel 16:46–48, where the prophets called Jerusalem "Sodom" for its wickedness and failure of justice. "Figuratively called Egypt" names the city as the place of oppression and slavery (Israel's paradigmatic experience of bondage under Pharaoh). By 70 CE (when Jerusalem had been destroyed), the city that rejected and killed its prophets (Matthew 23:37) had been judged; the great city carries the accumulated guilt of all opposition to God.
9: "For three and a half days, people from every people, tribe, language, and nation will stare at their bodies" — the fourfold universal-humanity formula (used for the sealed multitude in 7:9 and the beast's dominion in Revelation 13:7) now describes the audience for the witnesses' humiliation. Bodies left unburied was the ultimate dishonor in the ancient world — it denied the dignity of burial and exposed the corpse to decomposition and desecration. Psalms 79:3 laments that the bodies of the saints "were given to the birds of the heavens and to the beasts of the earth" (echoed in the eschatological bird-feast of Revelation 19:17–18). Tob Revelation 1:17–18 presents burial of the dead as a supreme act of mercy and religious duty. The three-and-a-half days mirrors the three-and-a-half years of their ministry.
10: "The inhabitants of the earth will gloat over them and celebrate and exchange gifts with one another because these two prophets had been a torment to those who dwell on the earth" — the earth-dwellers' celebration at the prophets' death echoes the prophets' description of their own experience (Jeremiah 20:8–10; Ezekiel 2:6–7) — the faithful prophet is always a torment to those who would rather not hear judgment. "Exchange gifts" — a festive practice (Esther 9:22; Nehemiah 8:10) connected to celebrations; the earth-dwellers treat the silencing of the prophetic witness as a holiday. This anticipates the false relief that the elimination of prophetic witness brings, which is immediately shattered by the resurrection (Revelation 11:11).
11: "After three and a half days, the breath of life from God entered them, and they stood on their feet" — the resurrection of the witnesses directly echoes Ezekiel 37:10, where the breath of life enters the dry bones and they stand on their feet (Ezekiel 37:10: "I prophesied as he commanded me... the breath came into them, and they lived and stood on their feet"). The valley of dry bones in Ezekiel represents the restoration of Israel; the two witnesses represent the witnessing community that appears dead but is raised. "Great fear fell on those who saw them" — the same fear that gripped the soldiers at the tomb (Matthew 28:4; Mark 16:8), the appropriate response to resurrection-witness.
12: "Come up here!" — the same invitation given to John in Revelation 4:1 ("come up here!") is now given to the witnesses; they are taken up to heaven in a cloud, echoing the assumption of Elijah (2 Kings 2:11: "Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven") and the ascension of Christ (Acts 1:9: "he was taken up, and a cloud received him out of their sight"). The cloud is the divine-presence vehicle throughout both Testaments. Their enemies watching them go combines the shock of the resurrection with the irreversible departure — the witnesses are definitively vindicated and permanently out of reach.
13: The great earthquake killing seven thousand and a tenth of the city collapsing — the earthquake is the standard theophanic judgment signature (Ezekiel 38:19–20; Amos 1:1; Nahum 1:5) and follows the resurrection-ascension as divine confirmation. Seven thousand killed echoes the seven thousand faithful in Israel who had not bowed to Baal in Elijah's time (1 Kings 19:18; Romans 11:4) — here the number is inverted (seven thousand dead rather than preserved), measuring the scope of the judgment. "The rest were terrified and gave glory to the God of heaven" — this response (giving glory to God) echoes the similar phrase applied to the beast-worshippers in Revelation 14:7, who are called to "fear God and give him glory." Whether this represents genuine repentance or merely fearful acknowledgment is debated.
14: "The second woe has passed; the third woe is coming soon" — the woe-counting formula (cf. Revelation 8:13; Revelation 9:12) marks the transition from the sixth trumpet (the second woe) to the seventh trumpet (the third woe). The interlude of Revelation 10–11 (the little scroll and the two witnesses) has occupied the space between the sixth and seventh trumpet, just as the sealing of the 144,000 (Revelation 7) occupied the space between the sixth and seventh seal. This structural parallel suggests that the interludes are not digressions but essential commentary on the meaning of the trumpet and seal judgments for the church.
15: "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever" — the seventh trumpet announces not a new plague but the consummation of divine sovereignty. The proclamation echoes Daniel 2:44 ("the God of heaven will set up a kingdom that shall never be destroyed... it shall stand forever") and Daniel 7:14, 27 (the everlasting kingdom given to the Son of Man and the holy ones). The use of "kingdom" (basileia) picks up the term that defines all of Revelation's political theology: YHWH's kingdom is the counter-empire that stands against and ultimately displaces all human empires, including Rome.
16: The twenty-four elders falling on their faces and worshipping (προσκυνεῖν, to bow down/worship) repeat the action of Revelation 4:10; Revelation 5:14; Revelation 7:11 — the elders' prostration marks each major moment of divine self-revelation in the throne-room. They are the most consistent worshippers in Revelation, always present and always responding to the divine actions with immediate worship. Their falling on their faces is the prostration of complete submission, the bodily acknowledgment of sovereignty that the Roman imperial cult demanded of subjects before the emperor — here redirected to the only legitimate sovereign.
17: "We give you thanks, Lord God Almighty, who is and who was, because you have taken your great power and begun to reign" — the divine formula "who is and who was and who is to come" (Revelation 1:4, 8; Revelation 4:8) here omits "who is to come" because the coming has happened: the kingdom has been established, the waiting is over. The thanksgiving is for the taking of power (eilēphas tēn dynamin sou tēn megalēn kai ebasileusas) — the divine reign has always been real but is now enacted eschatologically. This is the "already" of Revelation's eschatology: the kingdom is announced as having come.
18: "The nations raged, but your wrath has come" — echoing Psalms 2:1–5 ("Why do the nations rage... the one enthroned in the heavens laughs... then he rebukes them in his anger and terrifies them in his wrath"). The fourfold list of those who receive reward (servants the prophets, holy ones, those who fear God, small and great) echoes OT formulas for the inclusive community of the faithful. "Destroying the destroyers of the earth" — the judgment is perfectly calibrated: those who destroyed the earth are themselves destroyed; the lex talionis at cosmic scale. The earth itself is a category with moral standing — its destruction by the wicked is a crime against the Creator who made it.
19: "God's temple in heaven was opened, and within his temple was seen the ark of his covenant" — the ark had been lost since the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BCE. Jewish tradition preserved the expectation of its return: 2 Macc Revelation 2:4–8 claims Jeremiah hid the ark in a cave; 2 Bar. Revelation 6:7–10 says it was taken into the earth for preservation. The rabbinic tradition expected the ark to be revealed in the last days (Mishnah, m. Sheq. Revelation 6:1–2). Revelation locates the ark in the heavenly temple — not on earth but in the divine presence, which the earthly ark always was meant to represent. The theophanic accompaniments (lightning, rumblings, thunder, earthquake, great hail) echo the Sinai theophany, confirming that what has been announced is a new and climactic Sinai-event: the divine covenant kept and the divine presence fully revealed.
